elements.sent-2411-001: We study the capacity of loop-erased random walk (LERW) on $\Z^d$.
elements.sent-2411-002: For $d\geq4$, we prove a strong law of large numbers and give explicit expressions for the limit in terms of the non-intersection probabilities of a simple random walk and a two-sided LERW.
elements.sent-2411-003: Along the way, we show that four-dimensional LERW is ergodic.
elements.sent-2411-004: For $d=3$, we show that the scaling limit of the capacity of LERW is random.
elements.sent-2411-005: We show that the capacity of the first $n$ steps of LERW is of order $n^{1/\beta}$, with $\beta$ the growth exponent of three-dimensional LERW.
elements.sent-2411-006: We express the scaling limit of the capacity of LERW in terms of the capacity of Kozma's scaling limit of LERW.
elements.sent-2411-007: In this paper, we study the capacity of the loop-erased random walk (LERW) on $\Z^d$ for $d\geq3$.
elements.sent-2411-008: LERW was introduced by Lawler in \cite{lawler80selfavoiding .}
elements.sent-2411-009: It has since become a central object in modern probability theory.
elements.sent-2411-010: This is partly due to its many connections to other models, chief among them being the uniform spanning tree.
elements.sent-2411-011: In that context, the capacity of LERW has become an important object of study.
elements.sent-2411-012: The capacity of a set $A\subset\Z^d$ can be considered as a rescaled hitting probability of $A$ by a simple random walk (SRW) started far away.
elements.sent-2411-013: Through Wilson's algorithm, the capacity of a loop-erased random walk is related to the length and density of branches in the uniform spanning tree/forest.
elements.sent-2411-014: Using Hutchcroft's interlacement Aldous-Broder algorithm developed in \cite{hutchcroft2020universality , the capacity of loop-erased random walk played an important role in determining the scaling of several exponents for the uniform spanning tree on $\Z^d$ for $d\geq5$ by Hutchcroft \cite{hutchcroft2020universality and $d=4$ by Hutchcroft and Sousi \cite{hutchcroft2020logarithmic and Hutchcroft and Halberstam \cite{halberstam22logarithmic .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: In particular, a weak law of large numbers for the capacity of LERW was established in \cite{hutchcroft2020logarithmic .}
elements.sent-2411-016: A finite analogue of the capacity of LERW was also instrumental in establishing the scaling limit of the uniform spanning tree on the torus in dimensions $d\geq4$ \cite{archer24ghp, archer24dense, michaeli2021diameter, peres04scaling, schweinsberg2009loop .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Before stating the results in this paper, we first give a formal definition of the LERW and capacity.
elements.sent-2411-018: Let $\Omega$ be the set of finite, one-sided infinite and two-sided infinite nearest-neighbour paths in $\Z^d$.
elements.sent-2411-019: Let $\cF$ be the sigma-algebra on $\Omega$ generated by events depending only on a finite number of indices (we call those events cylinder events).
elements.sent-2411-020: Throughout the paper, all events and random variables will be measurable with respect to $\cF$.
elements.sent-2411-021: We will also need the definition of the left-shift operator $T\colon\Omega\to\Omega$, given by $(T\omega)(k)=\omega(k+1)-\omega(1)$.
elements.sent-2411-022: The loop-erasure $\LE(\omega)$ of $\omega$ is the path obtained by erasing loops from $\omega$ chronologically.
elements.sent-2411-023: To be precise, $\LE(\omega)_i=\omega_{\l_i}$, where the times $(\l_i)_{i\geq0}$ are defined inductively as $\l_0=0$ and $\l_{i+1 =1+\max\{k\colon\omega_k=\omega_{\l_i}\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-024: When $S$ is a simple random walk on $\Z^d$ for $d\geq3$, we write \begin{equation}\eta:=\LE(S[0,\infty)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-025: This is well-defined, since simple random walk is almost surely transient for $d\geq3$.
elements.sent-2411-026: For a set $A\subset\Z^d$, the capacity of $A$ is defined as \begin{equation}\C(A):=\lim_{\|y\|\rightarrow\infty}\frac{P_y(W[0,\infty)\cap A\neq\varnothing)}{G(0,y)}=\sum_{a\in A}\P_a(W[1,\infty)\cap A=\varnothing),\end{equation} where $G$ is the SRW Green's function on $\Z^d$ and $\|y\|$ denotes the Euclidean norm of $y$.
elements.sent-2411-027: We refer to \cite{lawler2010random for more background on the capacity, including a proof that the two expressions above are equal.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Throughout the paper, we let $W,S$ denote simple random walks and denote their laws by $\P^W$ and $\P^S$.
elements.sent-2411-029: We drop the superscripts if the law is clear from the context.
elements.sent-2411-030: As in the previous two definitions, we will use $W$ in the context of a random walk hitting another set and we will use $S$ in the context of a random walk being loop-erased.
elements.sent-2411-031: Although motivated by applications to other models, we study the capacity of LERW in its own right in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-032: We establish a strong law of large numbers (SLLN) for $\C(\eta[0,n])$ in dimensions $d\geq4$.
elements.sent-2411-033: In three dimensions, we show that $\C(\eta[0,n])$ has a non-deterministic scaling limit.
elements.sent-2411-034: These complement earlier results on the capacity of SRW, which has a rich history.
elements.sent-2411-035: Already in 1968, Jain and Orey established a SLLN for the capacity of the range of SRW in dimensions $d\geq5$ \cite{jain68range .}
elements.sent-2411-036: This was then extended to a central limit theorem for $d\geq6$ by Asselah, Schapira and Sousi in \cite{asselah18capacity .}
elements.sent-2411-037: The same authors also proved a SLLN and central limit theorem for $d=4$ in \cite{asselahcapacity19 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Furthermore, in \cite{chang17two , Chang showed that the scaling limit in $d=3$ is random.}
elements.sent-2411-039: We also give explicit expressions for the limiting values.
elements.sent-2411-040: In dimensions $d\geq5$, we show that the SLLN limit equals the non-intersection probability of a SRW and a two-sided loop-erased random walk.
elements.sent-2411-041: Two-sided LERW for $d\geq5$ was first constructed by Lawler in \cite{lawler80selfavoiding .}
elements.sent-2411-042: Much later, Lawler, Sun and Wu constructed two-sided LERW for $d=4$ in \cite{lawler19fourdimensional .}
elements.sent-2411-043: We show that (two-sided) LERW is ergodic in four dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-044: We then use an alternative formula for the capacity to express the limit in terms of non-intersection probabilities of a SRW and two-sided LERW.
elements.sent-2411-045: Both the ergodicity of LERW for $d=4$ as well as the alternative capacity formula are of independent interest.
elements.sent-2411-046: In three dimensions, we do not consider the two-sided LERW.
elements.sent-2411-047: Rather, we express the scaling limit of $\C(\eta[0,n])$ as a function of the scaling limit of LERW, which was first constructed by Kozma in \cite{kozma2007scaling .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Furthermore, we show that the correct scaling for the capacity is $n^{1/\beta}$, with $\beta$ being the growth exponent of three-dimensional LERW.

elements.sent-2411-148: We first prove a lower bound.
elements.sent-2411-149: By the definition of capacity and recalling that $\P^W$ is the law of a SRW $W$, we have \begin{equation}\begin{split} \C(\heta[0,n])= \sum_{k=0}^n\P_{\heta(k)}^W(W[1,\infty)\cap\heta[0,n]=\varnothing\mid\heta) \geq \sum_{k=0}^n\P_{\heta(k)}^W(W[1,\infty)\cap\heta(-\infty,\infty)=\varnothing\mid\heta) = \sum_{k=0}^n\P_{0}^W(W[1,\infty)\cap(T^k\heta)(-\infty,\infty)=\varnothing\mid\heta). \end{split}\end{equation} Since $\heta$ is ergodic and stationary with respect to $T$, it follows by Birkhoff's ergodic's theorem that almost surely, \begin{equation}\begin{split} \liminf_{n\rightarrow\infty}\frac{1}{n}\C(\heta[0,n])= \E^{\heta}[\P_{0}^W(W[1,\infty)\cap(T^k\heta)(-\infty,\infty)=\varnothing\mid\heta)] = \P(W[1,\infty)\cap\heta(-\infty,\infty)=\varnothing\mid W(0)=\heta(0)=0), \end{split}\end{equation} which settles the lower bound.
elements.sent-2411-150: We now turn to the upper bound.. Let $m\in\N$.

elements.sent-2411-302: Thus, by \eqref{eq:capacitysubsetZd and \eqref{eq:capacitysubsetRd , we have \begin{equation}\begin{split} \C_{\Z^3}(B(\eta[0,n],3\delta n^{1/\beta})\geq 3\C_{\R^3}(B(\eta[0,n],3\delta n^{1/\beta}). \end{split}\end{equation} The extra factor 3 comes from the fact that $G_{\Z^d (0,y)\sim3G_{\R^d (0,y)$ as $\|y\|\rightarrow\infty$, see \cite[Theorem 3.3]{morters2010brownian and \cite[Theorem 4.3.1]{lawler2010random .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-303: In conclusion, for $n$ sufficiently large depending on $\delta$, \begin{equation}\begin{split} \P\left(\frac{1}{n^{1/\beta}}\C_{\Z^3}(B(\eta[0,n],3\delta n^{1/\beta}))\leq(1-\varepsilon)^{-1}x,E(n,\delta)\right) \leq \P\left(\frac{3}{n^{1/\beta}}\C_{\R^3}(B(\eta[0,n],2\delta n^{1/\beta}))\leq(1-\varepsilon)^{-1}x,E(n,\delta)\right) = \P\left(3\C_{\R^3}(B(n^{-1/\beta}\eta[0,n],2\delta))\leq(1-\varepsilon)^{-1}x,E(n,\delta)\right). \end{split}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-304: On the event $E$, we have $B(n^{-1/\beta}\eta[0,n],2\delta)\supset B(\gamma[0,1],\delta)\supset\gamma[0,1]$, so \begin{equation}\begin{split} \P\left(3\C_{\R^3}(B(n^{-1/\beta}\eta[0,n],2\delta))\leq(1-\varepsilon)^{-1}x,E(n,\delta)\right) \leq \P\left(3\C_{\R^3}(\gamma[0,1])\leq(1-\varepsilon)^{-1}x,E(n,\delta)\right). \end{split}\end{equation} Furthermore, since we assumed $x$ to be a continuity point of the distribution $\P(3\C(\gamma[0,1])\leq\cdot)$, we have \begin{equation}\P\left(3\C_{\R^3}(\gamma[0,1])\leq(1-\varepsilon)^{-1}x,E(n,\delta)\right)\leq\P(3\C_{\R^3}(\gamma[0,1])\leq x)+o(1),\qquad\varepsilon\downarrow0,\end{equation} which completes the proof of \eqref{eq:lln3dupperbound .}
elements.sent-2411-305: The proof that \begin{equation}\P\left(3\C_{\R^3}(\gamma[0,1])\leq x\right)\leq\P\left(\frac{1}{n^{1/\beta}}\C_{\Z^3}(\eta[0,n])\leq x\right),\qquad n\rightarrow\infty\end{equation} is entirely analogous.
elements.sent-2411-307: Step 1 is also the same, only using that $E\subset E_3$, instead of $E_2$.. \end{proof.}
